coredata.dc:title: Machine learning-driven approaches for synthesizing carbon dots and their applications in photoelectrochemical sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadzadeh kakhki, Roya
Mohammadpoor, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been a subject of great interest among researchers due to their diverse physicochemical properties and numerous advantageous attributes such as good biocompatibility, unique optical properties, low cost, eco-friendliness, abundant functional groups (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) high stability, and excellent electron mobility. With the rapid advancement of data-driven technologies, machine learning (ML) has gained significant attention as a primary and indispensable tool in different applications in numerous research fields, including the monitoring of chemical reactions. By utilizing machine learning algorithms, the properties of carbon dots can be enhanced, such as fluorescence, stability, and electrocatalytic activity, as well as optimizing the synthesis process. Moreover, machine learning can be utilized to screen carbon dot precursors and predict their properties, providing various advantages in developing carbon dots with superior properties. As a result, machine learning offers numerous benefits in carbon dots synthesis, which has the potential to impact various fields. Photoelectrochemical sensors are a type of chemical sensor that use light to generate a photocurrent, which is then used to detect the presence of a target analyte. These sensors have gained significant attention due to their high sensitivity, selectivity, and low cost, making them a promising tool for a variety of applications in fields such as environmental monitoring and biomedical sensing. Due to their fascinating electronic and photonic properties, CQDs have gained considerable attention in the development of photoelectrochemical sensors. This review article provides an overview of recent advancements in the machine learning synthesis of CQDs and their applications in constructing photoelectrochemical sensors.

head.ce:title: Machine learning-driven approaches for synthesizing carbon dots and their applications in photoelectrochemical sensors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been a subject of great interest among researchers due to their diverse physicochemical properties and numerous advantageous attributes such as good biocompatibility, unique optical properties, low cost, eco-friendliness, abundant functional groups (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) high stability, and excellent electron mobility. With the rapid advancement of data-driven technologies, machine learning (ML) has gained significant attention as a primary and indispensable tool in different applications in numerous research fields, including the monitoring of chemical reactions. By utilizing machine learning algorithms, the properties of carbon dots can be enhanced, such as fluorescence, stability, and electrocatalytic activity, as well as optimizing the synthesis process. Moreover, machine learning can be utilized to screen carbon dot precursors and predict their properties, providing various advantages in developing carbon dots with superior properties. As a result, machine learning offers numerous benefits in carbon dots synthesis, which has the potential to impact various fields. Photoelectrochemical sensors are a type of chemical sensor that use light to generate a photocurrent, which is then used to detect the presence of a target analyte. These sensors have gained significant attention due to their high sensitivity, selectivity, and low cost, making them a promising tool for a variety of applications in fields such as environmental monitoring and biomedical sensing. Due to their fascinating electronic and photonic properties, CQDs have gained considerable attention in the development of photoelectrochemical sensors. This review article provides an overview of recent advancements in the machine learning synthesis of CQDs and their applications in constructing photoelectrochemical sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials have long been an important area of research in material science, with various applications across chemistry, materials, and other interdisciplinary fields. From traditional industrial carbons like activated carbon and carbon black to newer carbon nanomaterials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon-based materials are known for their environmental friendliness. However, these macroscopic carbon materials do not have an appropriate band gap, making it difficult to use them as effective materials. This is where carbon quantum dots (CQDs) come in. CQDs are a new and promising member of the carbon family, offering excellent and tunable photoluminescence, high quantum yield, low toxicity, small size, appreciable biocompatibility, and abundant low-cost sources. As a result, they have found important applications in various fields such as biomedicine, catalysis, optoelectronic devices, and anti-counterfeiting [1–6].

Developing reliable and efficient synthesis methods for producing high-quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with controllable properties is a significant challenge in the field. To address this challenge and improve CQD synthesis, machine learning (ML) techniques have been introduced. Artificial neural networks (ANNs) and ML have been employed to optimize CQD synthesis and improve their sensing capabilities. ML models were trained on a dataset of experimental parameters and reaction conditions, which allowed for the identification of optimal conditions for synthesizing CQDs with high quantum yields. Compared to traditional trial-and-error methods, the ML approaches were shown to be more efficient and reliable, demonstrating its potential in accelerating the development of CQD synthesis methods. These advances in CQD synthesis could enable precise control of their properties for a broad range of applications, including energy conversion, optoelectronics, and bioimaging.

Photoelectrochemical sensors (PEC sensors) have some advantages from their ease and low-cost instrumentation, as well as they are based on the effects of the interaction between the analyte and the photoactive material. These systems can operate with high sensitivity and low noise due to the different principles involved to excitation and detection such as low interferences produced at the detector by the excitation source. [7,8]. In the electrochemical methods light out as promising alternative for analytical purposes since they can be operated with low cost and they show results as good as or sometimes even better than many analytical methods already described in the literature. Among the most prominent methods that exploit the electrochemical detection by means of monitoring of electric current or potential are the photoelectrochemical systems, such as they have received the great attention of researchers in recent years.

A PEC sensor is considered a novel type of analytical device based generally on the photoelectrochemical properties of semiconductor materials. [9,10]. The investigation of the properties of applied materials is of great interest due to their applications in electronic, optoelectronic and photonic devices, such as transistors, LED light emitters and many others. In this sense, the development of photoelectroactive materials that can optimize the generation of photocurrent or photopotential has been an important strategy for the development of PEC sensors. [11,12].

Carbon dots with size less than 10 nm have been demonstrated to act as photosensitizers for enhancing the performance of heterogeneous photocatalysts, due to their adjustable band potentials, excellent and tunable light-harvesting ability, and electron transfer efficiency [13].

One effective approach to enhancing the photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity of photoactive semiconductor nanomaterials is through the use of carbon dots. By carefully designing and manipulating the carbon dots/support composite, a high photocurrent can be achieved [14]. However, there is currently a lack of fundamental understanding of how the interaction between these materials facilitates the reaction process, which limits their wide applicability in PEC devices.

Quantum dot (QD)-based electrochemical sensors have become a popular topic in technology due to the unique optical and electronic properties of QDs, as well as their ability to be functionalized with a variety of relevant molecules. These sensors can generate photocurrents sensitive to the chemical environment of the surrounding solution when QDs are immobilized on a conductive electrode. While optical transducers based on fluorescence, FRET, CRET, and other mechanisms are widespread, QD-based photoelectrochemical sensors are a relatively new and important branch of sensing [15]. These sensors offer advantages over other types of sensors, such as ease of operation and a means of controlling the desired reaction through impinging light.

In addition to the advantages mentioned earlier, there are several other benefits of using QD-based photoelectrochemical sensors. Firstly, QDs can facilitate charge carrier transfer between the conductive electrode and the redox agent through tunneling processes, leading to a fast response and high sensitivity. Additionally, QDs have low dark-currents due to the immobilization layer, which attenuates alternate electron transfer reactions [16]. However, under illumination, the QDs play a key role as photoactivators of the sensor. Furthermore, the broad absorption spectra of QDs allow for their photoelectrochemical sensor systems to be excited by a common white light source, enabling the design of simple, cheap, and portable sensor systems. QD-based photoelectrochemical sensors can also be easily extended to light-addressable sensors by spatially resolved illumination of a selected area of the electrode, which could potentially possess higher lateral resolution than traditional Si-based light-addressable sensors. Finally, QDs can efficiently chemically couple additional moieties to them, such as biomolecules [15,17]. Given these benefits, QD-based photoelectrochemical sensors have great potential for chemical and biological detection. While many reviews have focused on QD-based optical sensors, this review specifically investigated on machine learning synthesis of CQDs and their advantages and also specially the photoelectrochemical applications of QDs in detection, including the functional principle, advantages, and recent fabrication methods.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of carbon materials with small particle sizes and adjustable optical properties, making them highly intriguing. They have gained widespread attention due to their versatility and potential for use in various fields, including photocatalysis [18]. This area of research has received extensive attention in recent years, as it utilizes sustainable solar energy, which is both clean and virtually limitless. CQDs possess several attractive features, such as ease of synthesis, good water solubility, high photostability, high photo response, low cytotoxicity, facile surface functionalization, good catalytic properties, and adjustable excitation-emission characteristics [19,20].

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is relatively easy, but there are specific challenges that arise during their production. These challenges include the aggregation of nanomaterials, the tuning of surface properties, and controlling the size and uniformity of the dots [21]. To improve CQDs' performance, surface passivation can be conducted to adjust the functional groups present on the surface, which can enhance their quantum yields (QYs) by eliminating emissive traps. Doping CQDs with heteroatoms or metals, such as nitrogen, phosphorus, gold or magnesium, can also increase their solubility and electrical conductivity [22]. Although both bottom-up and top-down approaches have been used to synthesize CQDs, the former is more commonly used due to its cost-effectiveness and eco-friendliness [23]. Despite its advantages, the bottom-up approach has poor control over the size of CQDs, whereas the top-down methods are more expensive. Chemical and biological precursors are used for the synthesis of CQDs, which possess remarkable optical properties, water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness. These properties make CQDs suitable for use in various applications, including nanomedicine, biosensing, chemical sensing, bioimaging, solar cells, drug delivery, and light-emitting diodes. However, several challenges still need to be addressed for the extensive adoption of CQDs. These challenges include the need for accurate control over different synthesis parameters to achieve the desired structure and size of CQDs. To address this, a manufacturing process could be developed to increase QYs and the large-scale production of CQDs.

Carbon dot synthesis is predominantly carried out through trial-and-error methods. Consequently, developing a comprehensive strategy that establishes a correlation between the physicochemical properties of the materials and the synthetic conditions is highly challenging. The conventional method of trial and error imposes constraints on the exploration and enhancement of nanomaterials [24]. Furthermore this is a time- consuming, laborious and resource- intensive task. Utilizing machine learning has been proposed as a viable means to create and steer the production of CDs that possess specific characteristics.

Machine learning (ML), which is a form of artificial intelligence, has gained a lot of attention for its ability to efficiently process vast amounts of data in various fields. Its success in domains like recognition, physics, chemistry, biology, and materials science has made it a versatile and effective tool. One of its most significant advantages is its capacity to hasten the discovery of new materials by learning from past successes and even failures. The review performed by Tao et al. [25] delves into machine learning (ML) algorithms that are applicable to the synthesis of nanoparticles, while also emphasizing important techniques for gathering extensive datasets. their examination encompasses the ML-assisted production of various types of nanoparticles such as semiconductors, metals, carbon-based, and polymeric nanoparticles. Also they evaluate the current constraints, benefits, and prospects of ML-supported nanoparticle synthesis.

In [26], the authors develop a machine learning model for hydrothermal-synthesized carbon dots (CDs) that enables us to understand the connection between different synthesis parameters and experimental outcomes. Additionally, this model improves process-related properties, including the fluorescent quantum yield. They use various reaction parameters (such as precursor type, reaction time, temperature, etc.) and the corresponding QYs of the synthesized CDs. They assessed multiple regression models, such as XGBoost regressor (XGBoost-R), multilayer perceptron regressor (MLP-R), support vector machine regressor (SVM-R), and Gaussian process regressor (GP-R). To evaluate the performance of these machine learning models, they utilized three commonly used metrics: coefficient of determination (R2), mean squared error (MSE), and Pearson's correlation coefficient (r). They claim that their findings show that XGBoost-R outperforms all the other three models and is selected as the best model for their study. Fig. 1 is showing their ML results, that is the heat map of Pearson’s correlation coefficient matrix, and feature importance. The results of their study highlight the significant potential of machine learning (ML) in guiding the synthesis of high-quality carbon dots (CDs). This capability accelerates the advancement of intelligent materials.
Their results (Fig. 1.b) shows that the most important parameter in their experiment is ethylenediamine (EDA) and has the strongest impact on their experimental outcome.

Roodbar Shojaei et al. in [27] employed artificial neural networks (ANN) to model the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) through the quenching mechanism of cadmium-telluride quantum dots (QDs). They used various algorithms to train the ANN and determine the optimal model, including quick propagation (QP), Incremental Back Propagation (IBP), Batch Back Propagation (BBP), Levenberg–Marquardt (LA), and Genetic Algorithm (GA). Their selected topologies for all abovementioned algorithms were 4–11-1, 4–6-1, 4–14-1, 4–4-1, and 4–7-1 for QP, IBP, BBP, LM and GA algorithms, respectively. The generated models were statistically evaluated, and the selected model's ability to predict the mean diameter size of the nanoparticles was verified, as showed in Fig. 2 a. Their results revealed that the GA 4–7-1 model exhibited the most favorable statistical characteristics. They also evaluate the selected model by calculating other metrics such as maximum R-square (R2) and absolute average deviation (AAD) and showed that its R2 model is around 1, as shown in Fig. 2.b, implying that the model is significant. In addition, the RMSE value (0.00059) as well as the AAD value (0.0025) were small enough revealing the high predictive accuracy of the selected model. Also they indicate the importance percentages of their parameters which indicates that their solution concentration has the most impact, as shown in Fig. 2 c.

Dager A et al. in [28] utilized various machine learning techniques, including principal component analysis (PCA), multivariate curve resolution (MCR-ALS), non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), auto relevance determination (ARD), and soft orthogonal constraint (SO). These techniques were employed to offer a plausible explanation for the origin of the photoluminescence mechanism in the synthesized CQDs. By leveraging ML techniques, they were able to effectively handle and analyze the extensive photoluminescence dataset, ultimately suggesting the most suitable excitation wavelength for photoluminescence analysis. The application of PCA facilitated the objective selection of the optimal excitation wavelength for PL analysis. However, they recommend employing the NMF-ARD-SO algorithm for analyzing the PL mechanism due to its superiority over other ML algorithms.

Tang B et al. in [29] has proposed a regression model using carbon quantum dots synthesized through hydrothermal methods in order to improve process-related properties, specifically the photoluminescence quantum yield. Additionally, a progressive adaptive model is developed, which incorporates machine learning at the initial stage of synthesizing new materials. In this study, the XGBoost-r and PAM algorithms were utilized. Fig. 3 is showing their optimization and acceleration results of hydrothermal-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with XGBoost-r and PAM.

Senanayake et al. [30] employed machine learning, specifically an artificial neural network, to design carbon dots (CDs) with specific emission properties and to estimate the impact of synthesis parameters on CD properties. They created a database of examples of CDs synthesized by different precursors from a meta-analysis of 407 literature. Their study found that the choice of reaction method, purification method, and solvent had a greater influence on CD emission characteristics than the reaction temperature or time. Using various models, the best machine learning classification model achieved an accuracy of 94 % in predicting the relative color of CDs. Additionally, hybrid (two-stage) models combining color classification and an artificial neural network k-ensemble model for wavelength prediction through regression performed significantly better than a standard artificial neural network alone. Fig. 4 is showing their Machine learning models, their algorithm, and training workflows. Also Fig. 5 is showing their workflow as well as some plots of predicted versus experimental emission wavelength (nm) of CDs by selecting different folds of their database.

Luo JB et al. in [31] have proposed an efficient synthesis method based on machine learning to aid researchers. By utilizing this technique, the pre-planned conditions for CD synthesis can be rapidly and efficiently predicted, avoiding the need for futile synthetic experiments and improving the effectiveness of red CD synthesis. They used cross-validation technique by divide their dataset into 10 folds, 9 folds for training and 1 fold for evaluating the prediction model. Also they used Ensemble learning (XGBoost) model. Fig. 6 is showing their workflow as well as resultant ROC curve and confusion matrix.

Xing C et al. in [32] were employed machine learning (ML) algorithms to create CDs with predictable photoluminescence (PL) properties when excited at 365 nm and 532 nm wavelengths. The variable utilized was a combination of precursors and the target functions were the PL peaks of strongest intensity (λs) and longest wavelength (λl). Six ML models were tested, namely k-nearest neighbors (kNN), decision tree (DT), SVM, random forest (RF), AdaBoost and Bagging. They divide the spectrum data to training set, validation set and test set, accounting e.g. 64 %, 20 % and 16 %, respectively. Their findings show that the random forest (RF) model demonstrated exceptional accuracy in predicting the PL peaks. Fig. 7 is showing a schematic illustration of their CD synthesis and machine learning process, as well as their results of RF model prediction of λs and λl with the test set. (a) λs and (b) λl with excitation wavelength 365 nm. (c) λs and (d) λl with excitation wavelength 532 nm.

The advancements in machine learning owe much to the advent of deep learning, which employs deep convolution neural network (DCNN) for extracting information from input data (such as images and videos). Wang XY et al. in [33] have created a model based on deep convolution neural network (DCNN) that can predict the optical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) such as spectral properties and fluorescence color when subjected to ultraviolet irradiation. They collect and used experimental data from 170CD-related studies. Their findings indicate that DCNN has immense potential in assisting the synthesis of CDs. Fig. 8 is depicting some of their findings where they used excitation-dependent emission (EDE) and excitation-independent emission (EIE) properties as groups for prediction. Before using DCNN, they evaluate some of available ML models such as support vectormachine (SVM), k-nearest neighbor (KNN), random forest (RF), decision trees (DT) and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) and claim that although these methods show high training accuracy, their generalization ability on the validation set is not good.

A summarized table highlighting important parameters and achievements of machine learning (ML) that contribute to significant material morphology and structure in the context of synthesizing carbon dots for photoelectrochemical sensors is shown in Table1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs in photoelectrochemical sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots have been utilized in various techniques to enhance the sensitivity of immunological electrochemical sensors, aiming to amplify signals. This is primarily attributed to their remarkable surface-volume ratio, which enables effective signal amplification. Moreover, the biocompatibility of quantum dots when interacting with other molecules, coupled with their distinctive reaction characteristics and capacity for surface modification, makes them indispensable in these applications. Furthermore, quantum dots find utility in labeling different antibodies through conjugation methods that involve the activation of esters via bonds such as avidin–biotin interactions. Additionally, these nanomaterials can be directly incorporated onto electrodes and subsequently coupled with molecules like aptamers, antibodies, or antigens to specifically target certain analytes. Following the reaction, there is an observable change in the electrochemical behavior. By modifying electrodes with nanostructures such as quantum dots, the introduction of various functional groups enhances the accessibility of molecules. This facilitates binding events, such as adsorption, occurring on the electrode's surface. Consequently, rapid electronic transfer is facilitated due to the high conductivity of the nanomaterial, along with an increased number of surface-associated molecules resulting from the expanded surface area.

PEC sensor is recently developed for establishment of new analytical method since the advantages of simple operation, low energy consumption and high sensitivity. In principle, light irradiation on the surface of photosensitive materials produces photoelectrons and holes, inducing voltametric response with conversion of the photocurrent into a readable signal, and the process could be considered as the reverse expression of electrochemical luminescence (electrochemiluminescence, ECL) [34–37]. The current issues could be concentrated on the exploration of photosensitive materials for construction of photoactive electrode [38].

CDs exhibit distinct absorption behaviors depending on the carbon sources or synthetic methods used in their preparation [39]. Typically, they display strong absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region (200–400 nm) with an extension into the visible range. The absorption bands observed in this range are attributed to the π–π* transition of the C═C bond or the n − π* transition of the C═O/C═N bond. Notably, certain CDs that emit red or near-infrared (NIR) light often contain π-conjugated electrons within the sp2 domains and/or connected surface groups/polymer chains, which contributes to their absorption in the longer wavelength range of 500–800 nm [40]. Consequently, the absorption features of CDs primarily depend on the types and content of surface groups, the size of π-conjugated domains, and the variation in oxygen/nitrogen content within the carbon cores.

Hematite (a-Fe2O3), known as a highly promising material for photoanodes, demonstrates localized conduction of small polarons, leading to limited transport of minority carriers. Increasing the operating temperature has been observed to induce the hopping of minority carriers in metal oxide photoanodes. In a study [41] carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced into photoanodes to create composite photoelectrodes. First TiO2 layer was deposited on the FTO glass surface to reduce contact resistance between Fe2O3 and FTO glass. Then Fe2O3/TiO2 (FT) was soaked in the CQD solution to obtain CQDs/Fe2O3/TiO2 (CFT). Also, a layer of CQDs was deposited onto vertically oriented Fe2O3 nanorods, followed by a thin film of Co-Pi cocatalysts (CCFT). The introduction of CQDs improved the bulk charge transfer and the surface catalytic performance of Co-Pi OECs, leading to a synergistic effect and a photocurrent density of 3.0 mA.cm −2 at 1.23 VRHE. This approach represents a simple and efficient strategy for enhancing the PEC performance of photoelectrodes. The procedure for synthesizing the Co-Pi/CQDs/Fe2O3/TiO2 composite photoelectrode is depicted in Fig. 9 . Fe2O3 nanorods were grown on the FTO substrate with a TiO2 interlayer to act as a charge recombination barrier at the FTO/Fe2O3 interface using the hydrothermal method. Then, the Fe2O3/TiO2 substrate was soaked in a CQD solution to obtain CQDs/Fe2O3/TiO2. The electronic structure and optical properties of the CQDs were investigated using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and PL emission spectroscopy. The CQDs had an intrinsic light absorption edge of about 525 nm and an energy band gap of 2.36 eV. The PL spectra showed that the emission peak shifted to a longer wavelength as the excitation wavelength increased. The content of CQDs in the CFT photoanodes was 5.21 % based on EDS results. Finally, Co-Pi was deposited on the outer layer by photo-assisted electrodeposition to synthesize the Co-Pi/CQDs/Fe2O3/TiO2 composite photoelectrode (CCFT).

In order to comprehend the photothermal effect's dramatic impact mechanism, the charge transfer and water oxidation kinetics of the photoanodes before and after NIR light irradiation were thoroughly examined. Positive slopes of the M−S plots for all photoanodes indicate that the samples possess n-type semiconductor properties (Fig. 10 ). The electron densities of CFT and CFT-NIR electrodes were found to be 0.46 × 1020 and 0.57 × 1020 cm−3, respectively, with significantly enhanced charge density due to the photothermal conversion-induced enhanced electrical conductivity, which is expected to improve charge separation.

The charge separation and transfer processes at the interface between photoelectrodes and the electrolyte were investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measured under simulated solar irradiation and revealing the increased interfacial charge transfer rate induced by the photothermal effect of CQDs (Fig. 10). The dark J-V curves were used to reveal the electrochemical water oxidation properties, which reflect the improved catalytic role of the Co-Pi OECs induced by the photothermal effect (Fig. 2). In this work the CCFT photoanode showed a cathodic shift (420 mV) of the onset potential compared to the CFT anode, indicating the catalytic effect of Co-Pi OECs. The dark onset potential of CCFT-NIR was also catholically shifted when irradiated by NIR light, suggesting the enhanced electrocatalytic water oxidation properties of outer Co-Pi due to the photothermal effect of CQDs. The enlarged double-layer capacitance of CCFT-NIR photoanode indicates that the temperature elevation of the photoanodes induced by the photothermal effect can effectively expand the OER active areas, giving a reasonable explanation for the enhanced OER activity. (Fig. 10).

In order to assess the impact of CQDs' photothermal properties on bulk charge separation and surface reaction kinetics, the study measured the efficiency of bulk charge separation (hsep) and surface charge injection (hinj). Hsep measures the percentage of holes that reach the photoanode/electrolyte interface without recombining in the bulk, while hinj measures the percentage of holes at the interface for water oxidation without recombining on the photoanode surface, i.e., surface reaction kinetics. The study used 1 M Na2SO3 electrolyte as a hole scavenger and measured photocurrent densities with the removed injection barrier for minority carriers of the photoelectrodes. The results indicated a significant improvement in sulfite oxidation performance after the NIR light irradiation for both CFT and CCFT photoanodes. The photothermal effect clearly improved the hsep of pure Fe2O3 over the entire voltage region, indicating that more holes can arrive at the electrode/electrolyte interface to participate in the PEC water oxidation reaction. The FT electrode showed a low hsep of 12 % at 1.23 VRHE, which increased to 23 % for CCFT photoanodes, and finally to 31 % with NIR light irradiation. The hinj of bare hematite was 66 % at 1.23 VRHE, which increased to 88 % over the CCFT-NIR photoanode due to the synergy of Co-Pi OECs and NIR light irradiation.

NGQDs, or nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots, have gained recognition as a highly effective electron mediator owing to their ability to tune electronic properties, exhibit biocompatibility and possess favorable photochemical attributes [42–44]. In a work, a sensitive and selective sensor for detecting Ascorbic Acid(AA) was developed using a Z-scheme Bi2S3@NGQDs heterojunction, which was created via a simple hydrothermal process [45]. The NGQDs were incorporated into Bi2S3 nanorods, allowing for the separation of photogenerated carriers and resulting in a sensor with a wide linear range of 0.1–5 μmol/L and 5–1380 μmol/L for AA detection, and a detection limit of 36 nmol/L (S/N = 3). The sensor also demonstrated satisfactory recoveries for AA in real vegetable samples and commercially available vitamin C tablets. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have been known to be excellent electron mediators due to their tunable electronic characteristics, biocompatibility, and photochemical properties. In this study, Bi2S3 nanorods self-assembled on the surface of NGQDs via an in-situ growth method, resulting in the Bi2S3@NGQDs heterojunction used in the sensor. The sensor demonstrated excellent selectivity and stability for AA detection, even in the presence of other antioxidants and small molecules. The study provides a new approach for developing efficient sensors for the selective detection of AA (Fig. 11 ).

To compare the optoelectronic properties of various electrodes, a photoelectronic response trial was conducted. The results revealed that when exposed to visible light and 50 μmol/L AA, pure Bi2S3 exhibited a weak signal increment, while the Bi2S3@NGQDs composites, which have NGQDs embedded in Bi2S3 nanorods, showed a significant signal increase due to the separation of photogenerated carriers, as illustrated in Fig. 12 (A).Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were also confirmed the charge transfer processes. In Fig. 12(B), the Bi2S3@NGQDs-based electrode exhibited a smaller semicircle compared to the Bi2S3-based electrode, indicating that the Bi2S3@NGQDs heterojunction facilitated the electron transfer process on the electrode's surface. Additionally, the PL technique was used to verify this viewpoint, demonstrating that the PL intensity of Bi2S3@NGQDs was weaker than that of Bi2S3.

Interestingly, the sensor distinguished AA from other antioxidant compounds, with the photocurrent response increment of other antioxidants being less than 13 % of that of AA. This indicates that the PEC sensor based on Bi2S3@NGQDs can selectively detect AA.

As is shown in Fig. 13 , visible light excites Bi2S3@NGQDs composites, which results in the generation of holes (h+) and electrons (e-) in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. Subsequently, the e- in the CB of Bi2S3 transfer to the VB of NGQDs and then recombine with the h+ in the VB of NGQDs. This process leads to the creation of photogenerated h+ in the VB of Bi2S3, which then oxidizes AA to produce a photocurrent response.

In recent years, there has been growing interest in the use of quantum dots to modify TiO2 and enhance its photoelectric properties [46,47]. For example, Sun et al. used a sequential chemical bath deposition method to deposit CdS quantum dots into TiO2 nanotubes, resulting in a photoanode with significant PEC performance [48]. However, protective layers such as amorphous TiO2 and PEDOT are required to prevent light corrosion of CdS and the production of Cd2+. Therefore, alternative quantum dots are needed to modify TiO2 photoanodes. Carbon dots are considered a promising alternative due to their excellent optical and electrical properties, including electron storage capacity, photo-generated carrier transfer medium, and large specific surface area [49,50].

Researchers such as Bian et al. [51], Luo et al. [52], and Liang et al. [53] have explored the use of CDs to improve the PEC activity of TiO2, with promising results. However, the effect of CDs on the charge transport kinetics of TiO2 photoanodes was not known, hindering further improvements in PEC performance.

To better understand the use of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) in semiconductor nanocomposites, a study was conducted using TiO2 as a model semiconductor system [54]. The study showed N doping can effectively prolong the photoinduced carrier lifetime. Therefore, a photoanode by decorating the NCDs onto a TiO2 mesoporous film was prepared and was conducted a detailed structural and PEC analysis. The results showed that the nanoparticle composite possessed improved visible light response and charge separation/transport ability, resulting in a higher photocurrent value. In the work various methods, such as Mott-Schottky analysis, photoelectron spectroscopy, Kelvin probe, ultra-violet photoelectron spectroscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and in-situ photoluminescence quenching by time-correlated single photon counting were used, to evaluate the energy levels and present a detailed mechanism for the interfacial charge transfer process. This study provides insights into the interaction between NCDs and semiconductor materials and their role in improving PEC performance.

Fig. 14 illustrates the PEC mechanism in which electrons from the HOMO of NCDs and VB of TiO2 are excited to the LUMO/CB, respectively, under full spectrum irradiation. The electrons in the LUMO band of NCDs are transferred to the CB of TiO2 due to its deeper energy level, and the holes in the VB of TiO2 transfer to the NCDs where they can react with the electrolyte for oxidation reactions. The accumulated electrons in TiO2 then flow into the external circuit to the counter electrode where reduction reactions occur, leading to a higher measured photocurrent. Under visible light irradiation, a new absorption band is obtained through interfacial charge transfer, resulting in higher photoactivity. In this hybrid system, a photon excites an electron-hole pair on the NCDs with a subsequent electron transfer to TiO2, which is then transferred to the counter electrode and complete the PEC circuit. The hole in the valence band of NCDs provides a site for electron transfer from OH–, which is the electron donor in the electrolyte solution, resulting in the conversion of D+ from D with NCDs/TiO2 as photocatalysts under illumination.

N-CDs anchored TiO2 photoanodes were synthesized by Y. Han et al [55] using a one-step hydrothermal method. In-situ transient photovoltage (TPV) measurements showed that N-CDs can trap photo-generated electrons from TiO2, improving the efficiency of charge generation. The N-CDs also increased the conductivity of the TiO2 photoanode and enhanced charge separation and transfer efficiency. When compared to primitive TiO2, the TNCD-15 mg sample exhibited a ∼ 160 % increase in the maximum amount of photo-generated charge, a ∼ 428 % increase in the charge transfer rate, a ∼ 913 % increase in carrier density, and a ∼ 34 % and ∼ 66 % reduction in the impedance of the photoanode and its interface, respectively.The PEC cell schematic is shown in Fig. 15 a, in which incoming high-energy photons are absorbed by the photoanode to generate electron-hole pairs. Holes oxidize H2O molecules at the photoanode to produce oxygen, while electrons transfer to the cathode and reduce H+ to produce H2. The LSV curves of TiO2 and TNCD-15 mg in Fig. 15b demonstrate that the addition of N-CDs significantly enhances PEC performance, increasing the maximum photocurrent density of TNCD-15 mg by 2.2 times higher than that of primitive TiO2, and decreasing the onset potential (Eon) of TNCD-15 mg by 0.022 V. The effects of different qualities of N-CDs on PEC performance are further investigated and compared in Fig. 15c, using samples synthesized and tested under different conditions.

A potential reaction mechanism is suggested in Fig. 16 . Upon exposure to solar light, TNCD-15 mg generates photo-generated electron-hole pairs, with the electrons rapidly transferring to N-CDs and then to the counter electrode. The photo-generated holes remain on the TiO2 nanorods, reacting with H2O to produce O2. The presence of N-CDs facilitates faster electron transfer during charge transfer, resulting in increased charge transfer efficiency. Furthermore, the anchoring of N-CDs in TiO2 photoanodes creates an electron-trap effect that slows down the slow electron transfer process, thereby increasing the surface redox reaction rate and improving the surface charge injection efficiency.

In the other study [56] a new photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor was developed for the detection of caffeic acid (CA) using a composite material made of titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTeQDs) on a fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate. The PEC sensor showed higher photocurrent in the presence of CA when illuminated with visible LED light, compared to individual materials used for the composite. The TiO2/CNTs/CdTeQDs/FTO composite based PEC sensor showed a linear relationship between the photocurrent and CA concentration ranging from 0.5 to 360 μmol/L with a detection limit of 0.15 μmol/L, under optimized experimental conditions. The PEC sensor was successfully applied to determine CA in tea and soluble coffee samples using the standard addition method. The average recovery values for CA in coffee and tea samples were 99.9 % and 97.4 %, respectively, indicating the proposed method's accuracy. The PEC sensor also demonstrated good precision, selectivity, and reproducibility in the detection of CA. The proposed mechanism of CA oxidation on the TiO2/CNTs/CdTeQDs/FTO PEC sensor is illustrated in Fig. 17 . When illuminated with visible LED light, the TiO2/CNTs/CdTeQDs/FTO composite absorbs radiation, generating electron-hole pairs in the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB), respectively. The CA acts as an electron donor to the hole in the composite, providing a better photoelectrochemical response. The presence of CdTeQDs on the TiO2/CNTs nanomaterials is suggested to increase the electron lifetime in the CB, resulting in increased photocurrent during analyte oxidation.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising photocatalyst due to its unique property of a narrow energy band gap (2.7 eV) under visible light irradiation, as well as its advantages of being metal-free, inexpensive, nontoxic, and biocompatible. In a report [57], g-C3N4 quantum dots (g- C3N4 QDs) were developed to generate photoelectrons and enhance electrochemistry sensitivity. To achieve enantioselectivity of the sensor, a supramolecular strategy was employed using chiral multifarene [3,2,1] (CMF), a member of the multifarene family consisting of 4-(tert-butyl) phenol and 2-imidazolidinethione units. The CMF's chiral nature was achieved by introducing a cyclohexyl group on one 2-imidazolidinethione unit to produce a (R,R)-configuration macrocyclic compound. The high affinity of the cavitand to thyroxine guarantees sensitivity by forming a stable supramolecular complex that should produce noticeable photocurrent changes on the photoelectrode. The layer-by-layer decoration of g- C3N4 QDs and the chiral macrocycle on the surface of the ITO electrode allows for enantio-recognition ability and ultra-sensitive response, with each component performing its own function (Fig. 18 ). The g- C3N4 material provides high thermal stability in both physics and chemistry, making it an ideal candidate for photo-induced electrochemistry.

The results of fluorescence titration revealed a stronger affinity of the chiral macrocycle with L-thyroxine than with D-thyroxine. A photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor was proposed to enhance the sensitivity based on the g- C3N4 quantum dots nanoparticles' facile preparation. The ITO-g- C3N4 QDs-CMF electrode was fabricated with a stepwise coating of g-C3N4 QDs and CMF host on an ITO substrate, which produced notable discriminative photoelectrochemical recognition for the L/D-T4 pair. The ITO-g- C3N4 QDs-CMF electrode demonstrated good performance in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and recovery. The results indicated the potential value of the ITO-g- C3N4 QDs-CMF electrode for electrochemical response to thyroxine with enantioselectivity for the L-configuration, could be further applied for a photoelectrochemical sensor. The photocurrent characterization of the stepwise-constructed PEC sensor examined toward 1.0 nM L-T4 showed a remarkable increase in the photocurrent with the immobilization of g- C3N4 QDs on the bare ITO substrate, followed by the coating of the macrocyclic compound. The established PEC electrode demonstrated high sensitivity and photochemical response for D-T4 with almost similar performance.

The results indicated that the photocurrent increased with an increase in the incubation layer, and the peak photocurrent was obtained with a two-layer drop of nano g- C3N4 (Fig. 19 ). However, adding more of the macrocyclic host resulted in a decrease in the photocurrent, which could be due to more occupancy of the g- C3N4 QDs surface by CMF, thus decreasing conductivity and hindering electron transfer. Therefore, it was concluded that the thickness of g- C3N4 QDs and the amount of CMF on the surface of the ITO electrode were critical factors for enhancing the photoelectrochemical response. Based on these findings, a PEC sensor was constructed with a two-layer decoration of g- C3N4 QDs and with certain volume of CMF coating.

By analyzing PEC performance metrics such as photocurrent density, stability, response time, and selectivity, researchers can gain valuable insights into the strengths and limitations of various sensor configurations. For example, when comparing different electrode materials, it is essential to assess their PEC performance in terms of their ability to generate high photocurrent densities and maintain stability over prolonged operation. Additionally, the response time, which indicates how quickly the sensor can detect and respond to analytes, is a crucial parameter to consider. Selectivity, on the other hand, measures the sensor's ability to distinguish and measure specific analytes in the presence of interfering substances. By comparing PEC outcomes across different sensor designs, researchers can identify the most effective materials, architectures, and surface modifications for achieving high-performance photoelectrochemical sensors with superior sensitivity, stability, and selectivity. By evaluating the PEC performance metrics advantages and limitations of different ML techniques can be assessed. For instance, ML models employing deep learning architectures, such as convolutional neural networks (CNN), may exhibit enhanced PEC performance due to their ability to extract intricate features and capture complex relationships within the carbon dot structures. On the other hand, ML models based on support vector machines (SVM) or decision trees may offer faster training times and better interpretability, albeit with potentially slightly lower PEC performance.

In recent years, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also known as C-dots or CDs, have become increasingly popular due to their distinctive properties. These small carbon nanoparticles are typically less than 10 nm in size and have found diverse applications in various fields. In this work the synthesis methods of carbon dots with machine learning algorithms were investigated….

Due to rapid advancement of machine learning methods in different chemical processes, some available reports of ML usages in CQDs synthesis were analyzed and their ML methods and their benefits are collected.

Moreover, due to special characterizes of CQDs we reviewed application of these materials and also doped CQDs with other semiconductor materials in photoelectrochemical sensors. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess remarkable physical and chemical characteristics, such as a significant surface area, rapid electron transfer, superconductivity, and small size. These properties make them highly suitable for electrocatalysis applications. Moreover, the abundance of functional groups on the surface of CQDs makes it easier to prepare multi-component electrical active catalysts. The interactions among these components can further augment the catalytic activity by promoting charge transfer, which is crucial in electrochemistry.

Machine learning (ML) has emerged as a powerful tool for optimizing the synthesis of carbon dots, allowing researchers to rapidly identify the most efficient and effective methods for producing these nanoparticles. In the future, we can expect ML to play an even more significant role in carbon dots synthesis. As the technology continues to advance, it will become increasingly capable of predicting and optimizing the properties of carbon dots based on a variety of input parameters, such as precursor materials, reaction conditions, and post-synthesis treatments.

To develop effective CQD-based catalysts with desirable catalytic activity and stability for use in diverse fields, including electrochemical sensors, a combination of theoretical and experimental studies is necessary. The versatility of CQDs is evident from their application as a photo-electrocatalyst, which demonstrates their potential to be employed in unexpected areas. CQDs have also shown promise in green chemistry and clean energy production, which is encouraging. Overall, the future of CQDs appears bright and full of possibilities.

Roya Mohammadzadeh kakhki: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mojtaba Mohammadpoor: Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Roya Mohammadzadeh Kakhki is an Associate professor in analytical chemistry at university of Gonabad-IRAN. Her professional experience is in analytical methods, Green chemistry, electrochemistry, water treatment, nano catalyst and photocatalyst synthesis and characterization.
